Cragg, Gerald Robertson

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Gerald Robertson Cragg was born in Ontario, Canada, 1906. He authored many books and was the Brown Professor of Ecclesiastical History at the Andover Newton Theological School. He died in Ottawa, Canada, 1976. One can find an audio reel at Harvard Divinity School Library on a lecture he gave titled The Validity of Non-Episcopal Ordination.

did.unittitle: Photocopy of books read; Photocopy of record sheet
Crane, John R.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: John R. Crane was born in Newark, NJ, 1787. He graduated from New Jersey College in 1805 and entered seminary in 1809. He received ordination in 1818 and pastored in Middletown, CT, 1818. He received his D.D. from Wesleyan University in 1845. He died in 1853.

did.unittitle: Two manuscript sermons, on the texts Isaiah 48:22and Matthew 6:9-13
Dieterich, Albrecht

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Albrecht Dieterich was born in Hersfeld, Germany, 1866. He received his Ph.D. at Bonn, Germany, 1888 and completed his habilitation at Marburg, Germany, 1891. He served as a professor at both Giessen and Heidelberg, Germany. His 1905 book Mutter Erde brought him acclaim as well as his 1891 work Abraxas. He died in Heidelberg, Germany, 1908.

Fisk, Pliny

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Pliny Fisk was born in Shelburne, MA, 1792. He graduated from Middlebury College, 1814 and from Andover Theological Seminary, 1818. He received ordination at Tabernacle church in Salem, MA, 1818. He served as a home missionary in Wilmington, VT, 1818 and as a missionary with the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions in Syria, 1819. He died in Beirut, Lebanon, 1825. One can find his journal, book of extracts and records, and notes in the Columbia University Library.

Fitch, Albert Parker

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Albert Parker Fitch was born in Boston, MA, 1877. He graduated from Harvard, B.A., 1900, from Union Theological Seminary, S.T.B., 1903, from Amherst College, D.D., 1909, and from Williams College, D.D., 1914. He was the pastor of First Congregational church in Flushing, 1903-05, and of Mount Vernon church, Boston, MA, 1905-09. He served as the president of the faculty of Andover Theological Seminary, 1909-17. He was a professor of the History of Religion, Amherst College, 1917-23, and the Lyman Beecher lecturer at Yale, 1919. He was professor of the History of Religion, Carleton College, 1923-27. He was the pastor of Park Avenue Presbyterian church, New York, NY, 1927-32. He died in Englewood, NJ, 1944.

Ford, Daniel Sharp

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daniel Sharp Ford was born in Cambridge, MA, 1822. He was an editor and publisher, who along with J. W. Olmstead purchased the Youth's Companion in 1857 and transformed it into a flourishing publication. One can find his will (1898) at the American Baptist Historical Society. He died in 1899.

Gano, John

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: John Gano was born in Hopewell, NJ, 1727. He audited classes at Princeton. He served as a pastor for some time in North Carolina and then became the settled pastor at the new First Baptist Church in New York, NY, 1762. He served as a Continental army chaplain in the revolutionary war, 1776-83. After the war, he shifted his energies to educational endeavors. He helped found Rhode Island College (now Brown University), was a regent of the State University of New York, 1786, and was a trustee of King's College (now Columbia University), 1787. He died in Frankfort, KY, 1804.

Going, Jonathan

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Jonathan Going was born in Reading, VT, c. 1786-89. He graduated from Brown University, 1809 and entered the ministry at First Baptist Church in Providence, RI, where the famed Stephen Gano pastored. He received ordination in 1811 and took charge of a church in Cavendish, VT. A church in Worcester, MA, issued a call for Going to be its pastor, which he accepted in 1815. He remained there till 1836. He helped found the Worcester Baptist Association in 1819. In the educational sphere, he was a trustee of Amherst College. He served as a corresponding secretary for the American Baptist Home Mission Society for five years and edited the American Baptist and Home Mission Record, the periodical arm of the Society. Educational goals drove him to accept the office of president for the Granville Literary and Theological Institution (thereafter Denison University). He died in Granville, OH, 1844. One can access his personal papers at the American Baptist Historical Society and at Denison University Library.

did.unittitle: Three miscellaneous library notes; Jonathan Going correspondence list; Manuscript letter from Edwin Sandys to Jonathan Going; Manuscript letter from B. Willard to the Board of the Massachusetts Baptist Convention
Gordon, Albert I.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Albert I. Gordon was born in Cleveland, OH, 1903. He initially began his undergraduate studies at the University of Pennsylvania, but upon deciding to become a rabbi, he transferred to New York University, where he graduated in 1927. He graduated from the Jewish Theological Seminary of America with a Masters in Hebrew literature and received ordination in 1929. He obtained his Ph.D. in philosophy from the University of Minnesota in 1948. He received an honorary doctorate of divinity from the Jewish Theological Seminary in 1964. His longest tenure as rabbi was at Adath Jeshurun in Minneapolis, MN, where he served for 16 years. For some time, he taught Judaism at Andover-Newton Theological Seminary. He was an adept administrator who focused on Jewish education. He was a prolific author of both pamphlets and books. He died in 1968. One can find his various works at The Wyner Family Jewish Heritage Center at New England Historic Genealogical Society.

Gordon, George A.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: George A. Gordon was born in Aberdeenshire, Scotland, 1853. He graduated, B.D., from Bangor Seminary, 1877, and B.A., from Harvard College, 1881. He received ordination in Temple, NH, 1877. He was the minister of the Second Congregational church, Greenwich, CT, 1881-84, and of the Old South Congregational church, Boston, MA, 1884-1928. He also served as the Ingersoll lecturer at Harvard, 1897; Lyman Beecher lecturer at Yale, 1901; and preacher at the summer school of theology at Mansfield College, Oxford, 1894. He received an honorary D.D. from Bowdoin, Yale, Harvard, Columbia, Brown, and Williams. He also received an honorary D.LL. from Western Reserve University and Boston University. He considered his book Ultimate Conceptions of Faith, his best. He died in Brookline, MA, 1929.

Grafton, Joseph

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Joseph Grafton was born in Newport, RI, 1757. He preached at various churches and received ordination from the Baptist church in Newton, MA, 1788. He served as the vice-president of the Massachusetts Baptist Missionary Society, 1815-25, and later as its president. He served on the committee of the Evangelical Tract Society, 1817, and was a trustee to the same society, 1823-29. He served as the president of the board of trustees of the Newton Theological Institution, 1826. He involved himself in various missionary organizations. He died in 1836.

Green, Jacob

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Jacob Green was born in Malden, MA, 1722. He graduated from Harvard College. He received Presbyterian ordination for a church in Hanover, NJ, in 1746; he remained there in the pastorate for over forty years. He served as a charter member of the Trustees of the College of New Jersey, 1748-64. After the death of Jonathan Edwards, he functioned as the temporary head of the College of New Jersey. His book A Vision of Hell was his most popular. The New Jersey Provincial Legislature elected him to its fourth assembly in 1776. He fought for independence from Great Britain. He did this in many ways, such as publishing tracts like Observations: On the Reconciliation of Great Britain and the Colonies. He died in 1790.

Griffin, Edward Dorr

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Edward Dorr Griffin was born in East Haddam, CT, 1770. He graduated from Yale College, 1790, and received his Doctorate of Divinity from Union College, 1808. He was the first pastor of Park Street Church, 1811-15, and served as the Professor of Sacred Rhetoric at Andover Theological Seminary. He was the president of Williams College, 1821-36. He died in Newark, NJ, 1837. One can find Edward Dorr Griffin's letters at Dartmouth Library.

Hancock, Nathaniel

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Nathaniel Hancock was born in Cambridge, MA, 1701. He graduated from Harvard College, 1721, and became the minister of a church in West Tisbury, MA, for 27 years. He served as a judge of the King's Bench, 1761. He died in 1774. One can find a collection of his sermons, letters, and receipts, 1725-61, at Martha's Vineyard Museum.

Herrick, Everett Carleton

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Everett Carleton Herrick was born in Livermore, ME, 1876. He graduated from Colby College, 1898, and from Harvard University Divinity School, 1903. He received ordination in 1901. He pastored in Charlestown, 1901-14, and in Fall River, MA, 1914-26. He served as the president of Newton Theological Institute and as the president of Andover Newton Theological Seminary. Dartmouth College conferred an honorary doctor of divinity upon him. One can find his correspondence at the Maine State Library.

Hopoo, Thomas

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Thomas Hopoo was born in Owhyhee (Hawaii), 1795. He adventured as a sailor for some time, experiencing dangers and captivity. He was a missionary to the Sandwich Islands.

did.unittitle: Typescript memoir of Thomas Hopoo
Horr, George Edwin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: George Edwin Horr was born in Boston, MA, 1856. He graduated from Brown University, 1876, and from Union Theological Seminary, 1877. He received ordination at Tarrytown, NY, 1879, and pastored at Tarrytown, 1879-84, and at Charlestown, MA, 1884-91. He served as the editor of The Watchman, 1891-1904. He was professor of History of Modern Christianity,1904-06, and professor of Church History, 1906-25 at Newton Theological Institution. He served as the president of Newton Theological Institution, 1908-25, and as president emeritus, 1925-27. He received an honorary D.D. from Brown University, 1896, and from McMaster University, 1923. He received an honorary LL.D. from Colby College, 1915. He died in Belmont, 1927.

Hotaling, Edgar Gage

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Edgar Gage Hotaling was born in Wellsville, NY, 1916. He graduated from Brown University, 1935, and from Andover-Newton Theological Seminary, 1940. He received ordination in Providence, RI, 1940. He pastored in Palmer, MA. He served as a Navy chaplain.

Hurter, George C.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: George C. Hurter was born on the island of Malta, 1813. He served as an assistant missionary of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions in Beirut, Syria, 1841-64.

Jacobs, Bela

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bela Jacobs was born in Dighton, MA, 1786. He served as the president of the Massachusetts Baptist Missionary Society, 1826-31. He died in 1836.

Kelly, George Washington

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: George Washington Kelly was born in Greenbrier county, WV, 1808. He graduated from Ohio University, 1830, and received ordination, 1834. He pastored in Hamilton, MA, 1834-50, and died in 1898.

Kimball, Charles O.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: No biographical information is available for Charles O. Kimball. He served as the secretary of the Massachusetts Baptist Missionary Society, 1836-39.

Kirk, Edward Norris

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Edward Norris Kirk was born in New York, NY, 1802. He graduated from Princeton College, 1820, and from Princeton seminary, 1826. He worked with the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions. He served as pastor of the Second Presbyterian Church, Albany, NY, and as the pastor of the Fourth Presbyterian church, Albany, NY. His longest stint as pastor was at the Mount Vernon Congregational church, Boston, MA, 1842-71. He died in 1874.

Klein, Ernst E.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ernst E. Klein was the interim minister, department of church and society, for the American Baptist Churches of Massachusetts. No other biographical information is available.

Libby, Frederick Joseph

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frederick Joseph Libby was born in Richmond, ME, 1874. He graduated from Bowdoin, 1894, and was a teacher, 1894-98. He studied at the University of Berlin and at the University of Heidelberg, 1898-99. He was a Winkley fellow at Andover Seminary, 1902-04. He received ordination in 1905 and pastored in Magnolia, MA. He died in 1970.

did.unittitle: Typescript address "The War and Christian Theology"
Malcolm, Howard

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Howard Malcolm was born in Philadelphia, PA, 1799. He attended Dickinson College and Princeton Theological Seminary. He received ordination in 1820. He pastored in Hudson, NY, 1820-26. He served as the secretary of the American Baptist Sunday School Union and later as the deputy of the Baptist Missionary Society. He was a missionary in India, Burma, Siam, China, and Africa. Two works emerged from his missionary travels. He wrote Travels in South-Eastern Asia, Embracing Hindustan, Malaya, Siam, and China in 1839 and Travels in the Burman Empire in 1840. Due in large measure to these writings, Union College and the University of Vermont conferred doctorates of divinity upon him in 1843. Dickinson College conferred a M.A. upon him in 1842. He served as the president of Georgetown College in Georgetown, KY. He served as the president of Lewisburg University in Lewisburg, PA, 1851-57. He served as the president of Hahnemann Medical College in Pennsylvania, PA, 1874-79. He died in Philadelphia, PA, 1879.

did.unittitle: Manuscript sermon, on text Isaiah 33:20
Mason, Francis

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Francis Mason was born in York, England, 1799. He received ordination in Boston, MA, 1830, and served as a missionary with the American Baptist Missionary Union in Burma, 1830-74. He wrote various books on Burma and translated the Bible into the dialects, Sgaw and Pwo. He died in Rangoon, Burma, 1874.

did.unittitle: Letter from Francis Mason to unknown recipient; Manuscript writing "The Unicorn"
Cotton Mather; Benjamin Wadsworth; Benjamin Colman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Cotton Mather was born in Boston, MA, 1663. He graduated from Harvard College, 1678, and received a M.A. from Harvard College, 1681. He received ordination in Boston, MA, 1685, and ministered at the North church for over forty-two years. He became a member of the Royal Society in 1713. He died in 1728. One can find Cotton Mather's works at the American Antiquarian Society and the Massachusetts Historical Society. Benjamin Wadsworth was born in Milton, MA, 1669. He graduated from Harvard College, 1690, and received a M.A. from Harvard College, 1693. He was a preacher and joined the Fellows of Harvard, 1697-1707; 1712-25. He served as the president of Harvard College, 1725-37. He died in Cambridge, MA, 1737. One can find Benjamin Wadsworth's papers at Harvard Library. Benjamin Colman was born in Boston, MA, 1673. He graduated from Harvard College, 1692, and received a M.A. from Harvard College, 1695. He was the pastor of Brattle Street Church, Boston, MA. He was a member of the Corporation and Overseers at Harvard College. The University of Glasgow conferred upon him an honorary doctor of divinity degree, 1731. He died in Boston, MA, 1747. One can find Benjamin Colman's papers at the Massachusetts Historical Society and his various works at the American Antiquarian Society.

did.unittitle: Pamphlet "A Testimony against Evil Customs. Given by Several Ministers of the Gospel."
Meek, Frederick M.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Frederick M. Meek was born in Audley, England, 1902. He graduated from Mount Allison University and Yale University. He pastored Second Congregational church

in Biddeford, MA, 1932-36; All Souls church in Bangor, MA, 1936-43; and Plymouth church in Des Moines, IA, 1943-46. He was the 18th senior minister of Old South church, Boston, MA, 1946-73. He published collections of his sermons as books. One can find his sermons at the Congregational Library and Archives.

did.unittitle: Typescript sermon, on the text 2 Corinthians 9:6
Mekeel, Herbert Surface

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Herbert Surface Mekeel was born in Dayton, OH, 1904. He graduated from the University of Michigan, 1927. He attended Biblical Seminary, New York, NY, Union Theological Seminary, New York, NY, and Andover Theological Seminary, Andover, MA. He was an instructor at the University of Michigan, 1928-31. He received ordination in Swampscott, MA, 1935, and pastored in Swampscott, MA, 1933-35. He served as an assistant and associate pastor at First Presbyterian Church, Edmonton, Canada, 1936. He also served at First Church, Ottawa, Canada, 1936-37, and at First church, Schenectady, NY. He died in 1986.

Merriam, Edmund Franklin

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Edmund Franklin Merriam was born in Winthrop, ME, 1847. He graduated from Colby University, 1868, and entered business in New York, NY, 1868-76. He received ordination in Livermore Falls, ME, 1879, and pastored in Livermore Falls, ME, 1879-80. He served as the editor of The Baptist Missionary Magazine, 1880-1901, and he served as the corresponding secretary of the American Baptist Missionary Union, 1892-93. He also served as the editorial secretary of the American Baptist Missionary Union, 1893-1901. He served as the editor of The Watchman, 1901-12, and the managing editor of the Watchman-Examiner, 1912-16. He died in Boston, MA, 1930.

Merrill, William Pierson

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: William Pierson Merrill was born in Orange, NJ, 1867. He graduated from Rutgers University, 1887, and from Union Theological Seminary, 1890. He received Presbyterian ordination, 1890. He pastored in Philadelphia, PA, and in Chicago, IL, for sixteen years. He also pastored at the Brick Presbyterian Church in New York, NY. He died in New York, NY, 1954.

Mills, Samuel John

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Samuel John Mills was born in Torringford, CT, 1783. He graduated from Williams College, 1809, and from Andover Theological Seminary, 1812. He served as an exploring agent for Massachusetts and Connecticut missionary societies, 1812-13, and as a missionary and Bible agent, 1814-15. He received Presbyterian ordination at Newburyport, MA, 1815. He served the United Foreign Missionary Society and American Colonization Society, 1815-16. He was an agent for the Colonization Society, 1817-18. He died at sea in 1818.

Murdock, James

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: James Murdock was born in Westbrook, CT, 1776. He graduated from Yale College, 1797, and later received a M.A. from Yale College. He served as a pastor in Princeton, MA. He was a professor of Greek and Latin at the University of Vermont, 1815-19, and was a professor of Sacred Rhetoric and Ecclesiastical History at Andover Theological Seminary, 1819-28. He translated the Syriac New Testament into English in 1846 and published it in 1851. He died in 1856.

Nelson, Charles Adolphus

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Charles Adolphus Nelson was born in Iönköping, Sweden, 1860. He graduated from Oberlin College, 1889, and from Oberlin Seminary, 1892. He received ordination in Oberlin, OH, 1892, and served as a missionary for the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions in China, 1892-1933. Date of death is unknown.

did.unittitle: Manuscript letter from Charles Adolphus Nelson to Dr. Dabney; Typescript work "The Fiftieth Anniversary of the American Congregational church in South China, 1883-1933"
Nelson, Ebenezer

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ebenezer Nelson was born in Middleborough, MA, 1787. He received ordination in Lynn, MA, 1820. He pastored a church in Lynn, MA, 1820-27, and another church in West Cambridge, MA, 1828-34. He served as an agent for the Newton Theological Institution and as the secretary of the Northern Baptist Education Society. He was the pastor of the Central Baptist church in Middleborough, MA, 1837-51. He died at Lynn, MA, 1852.

O'Neal, Ellis E. Jr.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Ellis O'Neal, Jr. was born in Norfolk, VA, 1923. He graduated from the University of Richmond, 1946, and from Andover Newton Theological School, 1949. He served as pastor at Hillsboro Baptist church in Crozet, VA, and at Chamberlayne Baptist church in Richmond, VA. He was a librarian at Andover Newton Theological School, served on the Board of Managers of the American Baptist Historical Society, 1961-81, and was an archivist for the American Baptist churches in Massachusetts. He died in Richmond, VA, 2003.

Obookiah, Henry

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Henry Obookiah was born in Hawaii, c. 1792. He was one of the first students of the Foreign Mission School formed by the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions. He died in Cornwall, CT, 1818. He became known through The Memoirs of Henry Obookiah, penned by Edwin Dwight.

Orton, James

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: James Orton was born in Seneca Falls, NY, 1830. He graduated from Williams College, 1855, and from Andover Theological Seminary, 1858. He received ordination in Greene, NY, 1860. He pastored in Greene, NY, 1860-61, in Thomaston, ME, 1861-63, and in Brighton, NY, 1864-69. He served as an instructor of Natural Sciences at the University of Rochester, 1867-68, and as a professor of Natural History at Vassar College, 1869-77. Williams College conferred upon him an honorary doctorate, 1876. He died at Lake Titicaca, South America, 1877.

Pearson, Eliphalet

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Eliphalet Pearson was born in Byfield, MA, 1752. He graduated from Harvard College, 1773, and obtained a M.A. from Harvard College, 1776. He taught grammar school in Andover, MA, 1775-78. He was an instructor at Phillips Academy, 1778-86. He served as the second Hancock Professor of Hebrew and Oriental Languages at Harvard College, 1786-1806, and as a fellow of the Harvard Corporation, 1800-06. He served as the interim President of Harvard College, 1804-06, and as the president of the Society for Promoting Christian Knowledge, 1803-21. He was a founding member of the American Educational Society, and was a fellow of the American Academy of Arts and Sciences. He died in Greenland, NH, 1826. One can find Eliphalet Pearson's papers at Harvard Library.

Peck, John Mason

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: John Mason Peck was born near Litchfield, CT, 1789. He received ordination in Catskill, NY, 1813. He served as an agent for the Massachusetts Baptist Missionary Society and as an editor for the Pioneer newspaper. He served as corresponding secretary of the American Baptist Publication Society, 1843-46. He also pastored in Edwardsville, IL; St. Louis, MI; and Covington, KY. He died in Rock Spring, WY, 1858.

Scammon, John Humphrey

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: John Humphrey Scammon was born in East Rochester, N.H. on October 4, 1905. He graduated from Bates College, 1927 and obtained later his B.D. He received ordination at Weston, M.A., May 23, 1930. He pastored at 1st church, in Weston, M.A., from May 1930 to July 1936. He served as the associate librarian at Newton Theological Institution, 1936-1937 and as librarian and instructor in Greek, 1937 and instructor in Hebrew, 1942. He obtained a certificate in L.S. from Simmons College in 1937 and a S.T.M. from Newton Theological Institute in 1939. Death date remains unknown.

did.unittitle: A Visit to the Libraries of Five Representative Theological Seminaries
Sears, Barnas

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Barnas Sears was born in Sandisfield, M.A., November 19, 1802. He graduated Brown University, 1825 and received ordination at First Church, Hartford, CT, July 11, 1827. He pastored at Hartford, 1827-29. He served as a professor of Language at Hamilton Literature and Theological Institute, N.Y., 1829-33 and of Biblical Theology, 1833-36. He served as a professor of Christian Theology at Newton Theological Institution, M.A., 1836-48 and as its president, 1839-48. He was the secretary of Massachusetts Board of Education, 1848-55. He was both president and professor of Moral Philosophy at Brown University, 1855-67. He functioned as the general agent for the Peabody Education fund in VA, 1867-80. He served as the editor of three journals: (1) Christian Review; (2) Bibliotheca Sacra; (3) American Cyclopedia. He received two honorary degrees, a D.D. (Doctor of Divinity) from Harvard University, 1841 and a LL.D. (Doctor of Laws) from Yale College, 1862. He died in Saratoga, NY, July 6, 1880.

did.unittitle: Manuscript booklet, "Remarks of B. Sears on some of the more important works in the catalogue of his Library"
Shailer, William Hosmer

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: William Hosmer Shailer was born in Haddam, Connecticut, 1807. He studied for some time at a seminary in Wilbraham, MA. He graduated from the Institution in Hamilton, NY, which now is Madison University, in 1835. Upon graduating from the Institution, he attended Newton Theological Institution for some time. From here, Shailer became the principal of the Literary Institution at Suffield, CT, in 1835. The Third Baptist church in Saybrook, licensed him to preach, in 1830. While in Suffield, he received ordination at Deep River, in 1836. He became the pastor of the Baptist church, in Brookline, MA, in 1837. He served as the Secretary of the Massachusetts Baptist Convention, for ten years. He served as the Recording Secretary of the American Baptist Missionary Union, for thirteen years. He was also a member of the Executive Committee of the American Baptist Missionary Union, for seven years. He served as a Trustee of Newton Theological Institution, beginning in 1853, until his death. He became the pastor of the First Baptist church, in Portland, ME, in 1854. He also served as a trustee of the institution, known now as Colby University. He died in 1881.

Sharp, Daniel

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Daniel Sharp was born December 25, 1783. He studied under Dr. Staughton of Philadelphia, in 1807 and received ordination, to be the pastor of the First Church at Newark, N.J., in 1809. He remained there till 1812, till he became the pastor of the Charles Street Church, in Boston, M.A. He served in a leadership role at Newton Institution for eighteen years by being on the board of trustees. He served in similar roles as a fellow in the corporation at Brown University and on the board of overseers at Harvard University. Additionally, he served as the pastor of a church in Boston, for forty-one years. He died in 1853.

Stearns, Oakman Sprague

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Oakman Sprague Stearns was born in Bath, Maine, 1817. Stearns was a Baptist minister and educator. He received his education from Waterville College in 1840 and the Newton Theological Institution, 1846. Apart from academic posts, he was the pastor of three Baptist churches. He received ordination in Southbridge, MA, 1847. One church was in Southbridge, MA, 1847-54, another church was in Newark, NJ, 1854-55, and the last church was in Newton Centre, MA, 1855-68. He also served as an instructor of Hebrew at the Newton Theological Institution, 1846-47 and as the professor of Biblical Interpretation of the Old Testament. Colby College awarded him a D.D., in 1863. He died in 1893.

did.unittitle: Manuscript booklet, "Some Studies of the Book of Zechariah" (1984?)
Stillman, Samuel

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Samuel Stillman was born in Philadelphia, PA, 1737. After a slim classical education at Rind's Academy, he studied theology, under Oliver Hart, a Baptist minister. He also experienced conversion, under Hart's ministry. The First Baptist Church, in Charleston, SC, ordained Stillman as an evangelist, in 1759. He pastored on James Island, SC, 1759-61. Later, he pastored at two churches, in Bordentown, NJ. Thereupon, in 1764, he took the role of assistant minister to James Bound, who served at the Second Baptist church, in Boston, MA. The First Baptist church, in Boston, MA, in 1765, installed Stillman as pastor. He served there for forty years. Stillman had an interest in education, for he assisted in the incorporation of Rhode Island College (later known as Brown University), in 1764. He continued in this venture as a fellow and trustee. He preached the the eulogy for George Washington, in Boston, in 1799. He died in Boston, MA, 1807.

did.unittitle: Manuscript sermon on the text 1 Corinthians 15:24, 25
Stow, Baron

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Baron Stow was born in Croydon, NH, 1801. He graduated from Columbian College, in 1825. He received ordination, in Portsmouth, NH, 1827. Thereupon, he pastored a Baptist church. Thereafter, Baldwin Place Baptist Church, in Boston, MA, in 1832, called him to be their minister. He served there for sixteen years. After this, he pastored Rowe Street Church, until 1867. He died in Boston, MA, 1869.

Straton, John Roach

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: John Roach Straton was born in 1875. He attended Mercer University. Three years later, he became the Professor of Oratory and Interpretation of Literature. After this, he attended the Southern Baptist Theological Seminary. He received ordination as a Baptist minister, in 1900. He obtained a Doctor of Divinity degree from Shurtleff, in 1906. He was the pastor of the Second Baptist Church, in Chicago. He served for five years as the pastor of Seventh Immanuel Church, in Baltimore. He pastored the First Baptist church in Norfolk, Virginia, from 1913-1917. He ended his pastoral ministry at Calvary Baptist Church in New York. He died in 1929.

Stratton, Bessie M.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Bessie M. Stratton was born in Iroquois, SD, 1896. She received ordination, in 1958. She was the minister of the Church of the Lighted Window, in La Canada. She died in California, 1983. [not entirely sure, if this is the right person]

did.unittitle: Tholuck, Friedrich August Gottreu

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: August Tholuck was born in Breslau, Germany, 1799. He attended the University of Berlin. He served as an extraordinary professor of oriental literature. He also served as the ordinary professor of theology at the University of Halle. He died at Halle, Germany, 1877.

did.unittitle: Manuscript letter from Friedrich August Gottreu Tholuck to Sereno Edwards Dwight
Tribble, Harold Wayland

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Harold Wayland Tribble was born in Charlottesville, VA, 1899. He graduated from Richmond College (later the University of Richmond), 1919. He received Baptist ordination, in 1919. He graduated from Southern Baptist Theological Seminary, in Louisville, KY, receiving a Th.M., in 1922 and a Th.D., in 1925. Additionally, he obtained a M.A., in philosophy, from the University of Louisville, KY, in 1927. Later, he received a Ph.D., from the University of Edinburgh, in 1937. Later, he became an assistant professor of theology at Southern Baptist Theological Seminary, 1925 and then full professor of theology at Southern Baptist Theological Seminary, 1929. He also pastored various Baptist churches in Cropper, Christiansburg, New Castle, and Simpsonville, KY. He became the president of Andover Newton Theological School, Newton Center, MA, 1947. He received an honorary D.D., from Stetson University, in 1930. He also received honorary LL.Ds, from Union University, 1939; Wake Forest College, 1948; University of Richmond, 1949; Duke University, 1952; and the University of North Carolina, 1952. He died in 1986.

Tucker, William Jewett

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: William Jewett Tucker was born in Griswold, CT, 1839. He graduated from Dartmouth College, 1861. Thereupon, he taught school in Columbus, OH. He graduated from Andover Theological Seminary, 1866. He received ordination to be the pastor of the Franklin Street Congregational Church in Manchester, NH, in 1867. Later, he became the pastor of Madison Square Presbyterian church, in New York City, 1875. He became the Bartlett Professor of Preaching at Andover Theological Seminary, in 1880. He became the president of Dartmouth College, in 1893. He died in Hanover, NH, in 1926.

Tyler, William Seymour

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: William Seymour Tyler was born in Hartford, PA, 1810. He graduated from Amherst College, 1830. He studied for two years at Andover Theological Seminary. He became a tutor at Amherst College, 1835. He became a professor of Greek and Latin at Amherst College, 1836. Later, he became the Williston Professor of Greek at Amherst College, 1847. He also was a catalyst in founding Williston Seminary and served on its board of trustees. When Mount Holyoke became a full-fledged college in 1862, he also served on its board of trustees. He was also a trustee of Smith College, 1871. He received ordination from the Congregational Church of North Amherst, 1859. He died in Amherst, 1897.

did.unittitle: Manuscript letter to friend from William Seymour Tyler; Manuscript letter to Sir from William Seymour Tyler
Vaughan, Richard M.

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Richard Miner Vaughan was in Bangor, WI, in 1870. He graduated from Brown University, in 1895. He graduated with a B.D., from the University of Chicago, in 1898. He received Baptist ordination, in 1898. He pastored Warren Avenue church, Detroit, MI, from 1899-1901; First church, Janesville, WI, from 1901-1908. He was the dean of Pacific Coast Baptist Theological Seminary, Berkeley, CA, 1908-1909. He also was an instructor at Pacific Theological Seminary, from 1910-1912. While he was at Berkeley, he pastored First church in Berkeley, CA, from 1909-1912. He became the professor of Christian theology at Newton Theological Institution, in 1912. He died in 1954.

Wakeman, William Walter

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: William Walter Wakeman was born in Moodus, East Haddam, CT, in 1860. He graduated from Brown University, in 1887 and received ordination in Hampton Falls, NH, in 1890. He pastored churches at Hampton Falls and Seabrook, from 1890-1897. He served as a missionary, with the Boston Baptist Sunday School Association, in 1897. He organized a church, in South Medford, in 1897 and pastored the same church, from 1897-1900. He also pastored in Reading, from 1900-1903; was stated supply in New Braintree, MA, at the Congregational church, from 1904-1905; he pastored a Baptist church, in Ashland, from 1907-1910; he pastored at Bellingham, from 1911-1916; he pastored at Westwood, from 1916-28. He died in Exeter, NH, in 1935.

did.unittitle: Wales, Samuel

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Samuel Wales was born in Raynham, MA, 1748. He graduated from Yale College, in 1767. For a brief time, he taught at Dr. Wheelock's Indian School in Lebanon. Yale College made him a tutor, in 1769. He became a pastor, in 1770. He served as a chaplain in the Revolutionary Army, in 1776. Later, Yale College appointed him professor of Divinity, in 1781. In light of his new divinity professorship, he resigned from the pastorate, 1782. He died in New Haven, CT, in 1794.

Walsh, Gerald Groveland

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Gerald Groveland Walsh was born in South Norwalk, CT, 1892. He entered the Society of Jesus (Jesuits), in 1910. He received his M.A. and B.A. from Oxford and London Universities. He received his Ph.D. and S.T.M. at the Gregorian University, in Rome. He received ordination to the priesthood, in 1926. He was a professor of Church History at Woodstock College, 1929-34. He was also a professor of Medieval History at Gregorian University for two years. He served as the head of the department of Italian Studies at Fordham University. He was the editor for Thought, Fordham University's quarterly, 1939-49. He was also a professor of Medieval History at Fordham University. He died in Queens, NY, 1951.

Washburn
Manuscript letter to his mother
Weeks, William Raymond

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: William Raymond Weeks was born in Brooklyn, Connecticut on August 6, 1783. He graduated at the College of New Jersey in 1809; studied theology at the Andover Theological Seminary. He was ordained on February 16, 1812 and served in a Presbyterian church in Plattsburg, N.Y. from 1811-1814. He resigned this charge in 1814 and was occupied in supplying different churches and teaching until 1832, when he became pastor of the Fourth Presbyterian Church in Newark. N.J. He died June 27, 1848. He published Nine Sermons on the Decrees and Agency of God (1813), Scripture Catechism (1813), and Pilgrim's Progress in the Nineteenth Century (1849).

Wellman, Joshua Wyman

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Joshua Wyman Wellman was born in Cornish, New Hampshire on November 28, 1821. He received his B.A. from Dartmouth (1846), B.D. from Andover Newton Theological Seminary (1850), and D.D. from Olivet College (1868) and Dartmouth (1870). Wellman was ordained on June 18, 1851 and pastored several churches: First Church East Derry (1851-1856), Eliot Church Newton, Massachusetts (1856-1973), Malden (1874-83). He retired in 1883 and became a trustee of Philips Andover Academy and Andover Theological Seminary.

West, John

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: John West was born on December 6, 1711 in Bradford, Massachusetts. Died in 1741. Possibly the grandson of Thomas West, an early settler of Massachusetts Bay Colony in Salem. No further biographical information is available.

Whitman, Beniah Longley

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Beniah Longley Whitman (1862-1911) was born in Wilmot, Nova Scotia on November 21, 1862. He attended Worcester Academy, Brown University (B.A. 1887, M.A. 1890), and Newton Theological Institution (1890). He was ordained as a Baptist minister on September 13, 1887 and served at the following churches: Newton Upper Falls, Newton, MA (1887-1888), North Grafton, Grafton, MA (1888-1889), Free Street Church, Portland, ME (1890-1892), Fifth Church, Philadelphia, PA (1900-1907), First Church, Seattle, WA (1908-1911). Whitman was the president of two academic institutions: Colby College (1892-1895) and Columbian College (now George Washington University) (1895-1900). He died in Seattle, WA on November 27, 1911.

did.unittitle: Microfilm Personal Journal
Williams, Comfort

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Comfort Williams (1783-1825) was in Wethersfield, CT born January 23, 1783. Prepared for college by Rev. Calvin Chapin, he graduated from Yale College in 1808 and Andover Theological Seminary (1809-1810) without completing his degree. He was ordained as a Presbyterian minister in February 1813 in Oneida, NY. He served as a home missionary in NY and PA from 1812-1816, and then pastored the First Presbyterian Church of Rochester, NY (1816-1821); he was dismissed from the church and remained without a charge until his death on August 26, 1825 in Rochester, NY.

did.unittitle: Manuscript letter from Calvin Chapin to Comfort Williams
Wood, George Warren

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: George Warren Wood (1844-1924) was born in Turkey on January 1, 1844 to missionary parents. He attended Hamilton College (B.A. 1865), College of the City of New York (M.A. 1866), and Union Theological Seminary (1869). He was ordained as a Presbyterian minister on April 11, 1872 in Michigan. Wood served in home missions (1872-1876) and the American Bible Society (1877-1879). Then, he served as a missionary to the Dakota (Sioux) Native Americans (1879-1889) and home missions (1892-1893). He retired in Fairhope, AL and served as the treasurer of the Fairhope Single Tax Corporation (1908-c.1912). Wood died on January 21, 1924 in Fairhope, AL.

Woods, Leonard

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Leonard Woods, Jr. (1807-1878) was born in West Newbury, MA on June 24, 1807 to Professor Leonard Woods, Sr., professor of Christian Theology at Andover Theological Seminary from 1808-1846. Woods attended Philips Academy, Dartmouth College, Union College (1827), and Andover Theological Seminary (1830). He was ordained as a Presbyterian minister on May 12, 1833, at the Laight Street Church in New York City. At Andover, Woods was an instructor of Sacred Literature (1830-1831) and resident licentiates (1832-1833). There, he translated and published Knapp's "Christian Theology." Woods served as the editor of the Literary and Theological Review (1834-1837), professor of Sacred Literature at Bangor Seminary (1835-1839) and president of Bowdoin College (1839-1866). Woods received advanced degrees from Colby College (1839), Harvard (1846), and Bowdoin (1866). He died on December 24, 1878.

Worcester, Samuel

scopecontent.head: Scope and Contents
scopecontent.p: Samuel Worcester (1770-1821) was born in Hollis, NH on November 1, 1770. He attended Dartmouth (B.A. 1795) and New Jersey College (D.D. 1811); he was theologically trained by Dr Samuel Austin. Worcester pastored the Congregational Church Fitchburg (1797-1802) and the Tabernacle Church of Salem, MA (1803-1821). He was one of the incorporators of the first corresponding secretaries of the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions. He died in Brainerd, TN (Cherokee mission station) on June 7, 1821.

did.unittitle: Manuscript "Sermon by Rev. Dr. Worcester of Tabernacle Church, Salem on the death of Joseph Ropes, who died on March 25, 1816, aged nineteen"
